An immunoperoxidase stain for MIB-1 reveals a proliferation index of 16% in the more active regions of the tumor. The findings confirm an aggressive malignancy with significant necrosis and high proliferative activity.

The pathological analysis revealed a diagnosis of multicentric invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast, histologic and nuclear grade II/III. The examination of the left total mastectomy specimen indicated multiple foci of invasive carcinoma with associated ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), comprising up to 25% of the tumor mass. Notably, there was no involvement of the surgical margins, skin, or skeletal muscle. Additionally, the analysis noted the presence of calcifications and vascular invasion, and the non-neoplastic breast tissue exhibited apocrine metaplasia and columnar cell alterations. The sentinel lymph node biopsy from the left axilla revealed one node positive for metastatic carcinoma with extranodal extension, while the second sentinel and non-sentinel nodes were benign. Special stains demonstrated estrogen and progesterone receptor positivity, with HER2/NEU being negative.

The histopathological examination of the left temporal brain lesion biopsy and excision revealed a diagnosis of glioblastoma with an oligodendroglial component (WHO Grade IV). The tumor exhibits both astrocytic and oligodendroglial cellular morphology, with notable features of microvascular proliferation, mitotic activity, and necrosis. Margins were not explicitly stated, but the presence of tumor cells was confirmed in the sampled sections. Ancillary testing included fluorescence in situ hybridization (FISH), which demonstrated deletions of chromosomes 1p36 and 19q13, and amplification of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) gene. The MGMT promoter was found to be unmethylated, suggesting a potentially less favorable response to temozolomide therapy. There was no mention of lymph node involvement, which is typically irrelevant in primary central nervous system tumors because they do not metastasize to lymph nodes. Further data correlation with clinical findings and additional molecular tests is recommended to reinforce the diagnosis and guide therapeutic decision-making.

The pathological examination of the right frontal brain biopsy revealed a diagnosis of Glioblastoma Multiforme (WHO grade IV) with substantial tumor cellularity and variable degrees of necrosis across different samples. The tumor demonstrated extensive necrosis, ranging from 10% to 50%, and high cellularity, predominantly around 90%. Notably, immunohistochemical analysis indicated that 40% of the tumor cells stained positive for MGMT, suggesting a potentially diminished response to Temozolomide (Temodar). Loss of PTEN expression was observed, indicating a reduced likelihood of response to EGFR kinase inhibitors, while elevated expression of laminin beta 1 and down-regulation of laminin beta 2 were indicative of more aggressive tumor behavior and a worse prognosis. Additionally, a significant proportion of tumor cells were immunoreactive to phosphorylated p42/44 MAPK (pMAPK), suggesting relative resistance to radiation therapy. The microscopic evaluation confirmed a necrotizing malignant neoplasm with densely cellular proliferation of pleomorphic, hyperchromatic, elongate nuclei, numerous mitotic figures, endothelial proliferation, and palisading necrosis. The gross examination of the tumor samples showed irregular, hemorrhagic, and soft tissue consistent with the description of glioblastoma multiforme.

The pathological analysis identifies the diagnosis of Glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) in the left parietal lobe. The tumor is characterized by significant cytologic pleomorphism, increased mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, and pseudopalisading necrosis. The mass, which shows peripheral enhancement and broad dural contact, also exhibits focal necrosis and hemorrhage upon gross examination. Intraoperative consultation confirmed the presence of a high-grade glioma. Microscopic examination further substantiated the diagnosis by identifying malignant astrocytic infiltration. Margins were not explicitly stated as clear or involved. Lymph node involvement was not assessed in this cranial specimen. Additional cytogenetic and immunohistochemical analyses revealed polysomy of Chromosome 7, significant EGFR amplification, centromere loss in Chromosomes 9 and 10, and allelic loss of PTEN, all of which are associated with poor prognosis. A high proliferation index was noted, and the tumor cells showed various levels of positivity for markers such as EGFR and MAPK while being negative for others like MGMT and AKT. These findings indicate a highly aggressive tumor with molecular features consistent with poor survival outcomes.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma in the right parieto-occipital region, confirmed through biopsy and resection specimens. The tumor exhibits characteristic features of glioblastomas, such as a neoplastic proliferation of astrocytes with pronounced nuclear anaplasia, frequent mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, and regions of necrosis. Notably, pseudosarcoma zones and entrapped neurons are also present. Immunohistochemical staining indicates sparse glial fibrillogenesis in the denser tumor areas and a slightly more abundant presence in looser regions. The MIB-1 proliferation index demonstrates a 19% rate in active tumor zones, highlighting significant proliferative activity. While the margin status and lymph node involvement are not directly assessed in this analysis, the aggressive pathological features observed are consistent with the clinical history of recurrent glioblastoma.

The pathological diagnosis reveals a glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) in the left frontal brain region. This high-grade glioma exhibits significant pleomorphism, frequent mitotic figures, vascular proliferation, and necrosis, consistent with the small cell variant of glioblastoma. Immunohistochemical analysis shows strong diffuse p53 positivity, and the Ki-67 labeling index is approximately 25%, indicating high proliferative activity. Molecular studies show a methylated MGMT promoter, suggesting potential responsiveness to certain chemotherapeutic agents, while no evidence of the EGFRvIII mutation is detected. The tumor specimen was thoroughly examined, and tissue sections confirm the presence of these aggressive features. No further mention of margin status or lymph node involvement is provided, which might be due to the nature of the brain biopsy and excision, focusing specifically on the primary tumor characteristics.

The pathological examination revealed a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme from the brain biopsy of the right partial tumor. The tumor is characterized by significant necrosis, anaplastic astrocytes, and marked vascular proliferation. Additionally, there is a notable presence of macrophages and mononuclear cells, likely attributable to the previous biopsy procedure. The MIB-1 labeling index is approximately 10%, indicating the proliferative activity of the tumor cells. The specimens consisted of multiple small tissue fragments, and the frozen section analysis confirmed the presence of a glioma.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of glioblastoma, classified as WHO Grade IV. The tumor exhibits significant malignant characteristics such as cytologic pleomorphism, frequent mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, and pseudopalisading necrosis. Notably, subarachnoid infiltration is observed in one of the tissue blocks, suggesting an aggressive and infiltrative nature. Genetic analysis reveals polysomy of chromosome 7 and 19, amplification of EGFR, loss of the centromere of chromosome 10 and PTEN allelic loss, and loss of 9p21 among other genetic alterations. Immunohistochemical studies indicate a high proliferation index and significant loss of PTEN, with positive staining for EGFR and MAPK. VEGF and MGMT are negative, and macrophage infiltration is prominent in areas of necrosis. These findings collectively underscore a highly aggressive tumor with poor prognosis. The margin status is not explicitly mentioned, but the tumor's infiltrative nature suggests that achieving clear margins may be challenging. Lymph node involvement is not addressed within the provided information.

The pathological findings indicate a diagnosis of glioblastoma involving four distinct excised frontal brain lesions. The tumors present varied characteristics including focal, predominantly solid, mostly infiltrative, and a combination of solid and infiltrative patterns. All excised tissues display widespread neoplastic infiltration of cerebral cortex and white matter, characterized by glial cell proliferation, significant nuclear atypia, and marked mitotic activity. Notably, focal areas of microvascular proliferation and necrosis are also observed. The neoplasm is consistently identified across all specimens, effectively confirming its extensive and aggressive nature. Margin status was not explicitly stated, and lymph node involvement is not applicable in this context. The pathological analysis underscores the pervasive and infiltrative nature of the glioblastoma, necessitating comprehensive management.

The pathological examination of the left breast mass reveals an invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC), classified as poorly differentiated with histologic and nuclear grades III/III. The IDC is accompanied by focal ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS), which constitutes less than a quarter of the total tumor and exhibits solid and cribriform patterns with intermediate to high nuclear grade and necrosis. The sentinel lymph node analysis from the left axilla shows one node with a microscopic cluster of tumor cells within the capsular lymphatic vessel, detected via cytokeratin stains, while the remaining nodes are benign with no evidence of metastatic involvement as confirmed by both H&E and immunohistochemical staining. Margins from the excised left breast, spanning superior, medial, inferior, lateral, and deep, are evaluated, but specific details on margin status are not provided in this report. Special studies, including the HER2/CEP 17 ratio, indicate no amplification of the HER2 gene, confirming that the Her-2/neu gene is not amplified. This comprehensive pathological assessment suggests a detailed profile of the tumor without signs of extensive metastasis in the lymph nodes and a non-amplified HER2 status.

A pathological analysis of brain tissue from a craniotomy in a female patient with a right temporal brain tumor reveals the diagnosis of glioblastoma, WHO Grade IV. The tumor characteristically demonstrates a small cell anaplastic proliferation of astrocytic cells, with notable vascular proliferation and pseudopalisading necrosis. Gross examination identified diffuse surface hemorrhage without a well-demarcated distinction between white and gray matter. Chromosomal analysis through FISH highlights polysomy of chromosome 7, EGFR amplification, loss of chromosome 10 centromere, PTEN allelic loss, and loss of 9p21, among other markers, indicating a higher hazard of short survival. Immunohistochemical labeling indicates a high proliferation index with 20% of tumor cells showing staining and positive markers for MGMT, EGFR wt, and MAPK. PTEN loss and partial positivity for EGFR vIII, but negative results for S6 and AKT, were also observed. No significant mass effect or vasogenic edema was noted in imaging, and no other abnormal signal intensities were detected in the brain parenchyma.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of glioblastoma in the right temporal lobe. This highly malignant tumor is characterized by glial neoplastic proliferation with nuclear anaplasia, numerous mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, and necrosis. Two biopsy specimens from the right temporal tumor (Parts B and C) confirmed extensive infiltration by glioblastoma. Gross examination showed that the specimens consisted of grayish-pink and tan-brown tissue with neoplastic characteristics. An examination of the posterior resection margin (Part D) indicated that the cerebral cortex and adjacent white matter were free of neoplasm, ensuring negative margins. Immunohistochemistry results indicated a MIB-1 proliferation index of 14% and confirmed the gliofibrillogenesis with frequent gemistocytes. Approximately 3% of the tumor nuclei expressed mutant p53 protein, and there was no evidence of a methylated MGMT promoter. Together, these findings confirm the diagnosis of glioblastoma without evidence of residual tumor at the resection margin. The temporal lobectomy (Part A) did not reveal any diagnostic features except for focal areas of recent edema.

The pathological assessment confirms a diagnosis of glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) located in the right temporal-occipital region of the brain, which extends medially along the splenium of the corpus callosum. Microscopically, the neoplasm is characterized by malignant astrocytic cells exhibiting cytologic pleomorphism, frequent mitoses, microvascular proliferation, and pseudopalisading necrosis. The margins of resection are not explicitly stated, indicating a need for clinical correlation regarding margin status. Lymph node involvement is not applicable as this diagnosis pertains to a primary brain tumor without evidence of extracerebral metastatic spread. Additional molecular studies revealed polysomy of Chromosome 7 and EGFR in 56% of tumor cells, along with allelic loss of PTEN and centromere loss of Chromosome 10 in 33% of tumor cells. Immunohistochemical analysis showed a high proliferation index with significant staining of EGFR wild type, S6, AKT, and MAPK markers. Positive MGMT promoter methylation was identified, suggesting a potential prognostic and therapeutic implication. Overall, the findings indicate a highly aggressive tumor with multiple genetic aberrations, contributing to a poor prognosis despite the presence of intact PTEN protein by immunohistochemistry.

The pathological findings confirm a diagnosis of glioblastoma involving the medial temporal, hippocampus, and posterior brain regions. The tumor displays extensive areas of necrosis, constituting approximately 90% of the tissue, interspersed with neoplastic astrocytic proliferation exhibiting mitotic figures, microvascular proliferation, and microvessel thrombosis. The MIB-1 proliferation index peaks at 15% in the more active regions. In the hippocampus, the tumor shows sparse infiltration with microscopic evidence of subependymal spread. Immunohistochemical analysis reveals significant glial fibrillogenesis, minimal viable phagocytic activity, and pronounced abnormal angiogenesis marked by perivascular cellular proliferation. Molecular testing for MGMT promoter methylation indicates a mix of positive and negative results across different specimens. No margin status or lymph node evaluation is provided in the report, which focuses on the histological and molecular landscape of a high-grade astrocytic neoplasm.

The pathological examination of the brain excisional biopsies reveals the diagnosis of glioblastoma, WHO grade IV/IV, involving both the right frontotemporal region and a superficial brain area. The tumors display high histological grade with significant cellularity, characterized by medium-sized nuclei exhibiting prominent anaplasia, scant cytoplasm, and a fibrillary background. Frequent karyorrhectic nuclei are evident, alongside occasional mitotic figures. The right frontotemporal tumor shows notable microvascular proliferation and foci of tumor necrosis associated with vascular necrosis and thrombosis, while the superficial biopsy lacks microvascular proliferation. Immunohistochemical staining reveals scant fibrilogenesis by neoplastic cells (GFAP), a dense network of microvessels with evidence of cellular proliferation (factor VIII), and a high proliferation index of 35% (MIB-1). No status on margin involvement or lymph node involvement is provided, as is typical for primary central nervous system tumors. These findings are consistent with the aggressive nature of glioblastoma, highlighting its poor prognosis and necessitating comprehensive clinical management.

The patient, a female, underwent a wide excision of two foci of carcinoma in the right breast and sentinel lymph node biopsy. Pathological analysis revealed invasive ductal carcinoma of no special type (NOS), histologic and nuclear grade III/III, present as three separate nodules, accompanied by ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) of solid type, high nuclear grade, and minimal necrosis which constitutes less than 25% of the total tumor mass. The invasive carcinoma was notably close to the surgical margins, being 0.15 cm from the superior margin and 0.4 cm from the posterior margin, although no vascular invasion was identified. Sentinel lymph nodes examined were initially deemed benign, but further investigation revealed microscopic metastasis in one node and isolated cytokeratin-positive cells in another. Immunohistochemical staining showed that the invasive carcinoma was estrogen receptor (ER) positive in over 95% of cells with strong intensity, progesterone receptor (PR) positive in 40% of cells with moderate intensity, and HER2/neu negative. The non-neoplastic breast tissue was unremarkable and no calcifications were found in either the invasive or in situ carcinoma.

The pathological findings reveal a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme (GBM) in the left parietal region of the brain. The tumor is characterized by high cellularity and anaplastic features, including a focal giant cell pattern. Extensive necrosis, microvascular proliferation, and mitotic activity are observed, which are typical features of glioblastoma. The excised tumor exhibits a hemorrhagic mass. No specific measurements are provided regarding margin status or lymph node involvement in this report, which is consistent with the localized nature of primary brain tumors. Therefore, the comprehensive microscopic analysis confirms the presence of a highly aggressive glioblastoma multiforme, necessitating further clinical management.

The pathological examination of the right parietal brain biopsy and resection reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma, characterized by diffuse infiltration and extensive effacement of the cerebral tissue by an astrocytic neoplastic proliferation. The tumor exhibits significant nuclear anaplasia, numerous mitotic figures, prominent microvascular proliferation, and areas of necrosis. Multinucleated cells are present within the specimen. Immunohistochemistry findings show glial fibrillogenesis as indicated by GFAP staining, with no evidence of a neoplastic neuronal component. Approximately 1% of tumor cells overexpress the p53 protein, and the MIB-1 proliferation index is around 21% in the more active areas, confirming a high histological grade glioma. Importantly, methylated MGMT promoter is detected, which holds prognostic and therapeutic implications. The resection specimen margins status and lymph node involvement were not specifically addressed in this report. The gross examination noted semi-firm, tannish-grey, glassy fragments of the tumor, consistent with glioblastoma features.

The excision biopsy specimens from the brain reveal a diagnosis of Glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV). The tumor is a high-grade glial neoplasm characterized by elongated and angulated nuclei with gemistocytes, frequent mitotic figures, and prominent vascular proliferation. Immunohistochemistry shows diffuse p53 positivity, and the Ki-67 labeling index is markedly elevated at approximately 80%, indicating high proliferative activity. The margins of the resected tissue are not explicitly mentioned, suggesting the analysis is focused primarily on the tumor's intrinsic characteristics. Lymph node involvement is not addressed, which is consistent with typical glioblastoma assessment centered on the primary tumor site. Additionally, molecular testing reveals a methylated MGMT promoter, which is significant for therapeutic considerations, as patients with this molecular profile often respond better to alkylating agents. Grossly, the specimens include both hemorrhagic and tan friable tissue fragments, processed for further examination, with sections showing atypical glial proliferation consistent with glioma, aligning with the final diagnosis.

The pathological evaluation of the right parietal brain tumor revealed a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme. This highly aggressive and malignant tumor was identified in both the intraoperative frozen section and the formalin-fixed tissue samples. Gross examination of the specimens showed multiple fragments of soft, hemorrhagic tissue displaying characteristic brown-red and grey coloration. All submitted tissue sections confirmed the presence of glioblastoma multiforme, marked by significant cellular atypia, high mitotic activity, necrosis, and microvascular proliferation. Margin status was not explicitly mentioned, indicating potential uncertainty regarding tumor resection completeness. Additionally, there was no documentation of lymph node involvement, which is consistent with the typical presentation of glioblastoma confined to the central nervous system.

The pathological diagnosis reveals a glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) in the right temporal lobe of the brain, characterized by gemistocytic astrocytic features, vascular proliferation, and pseudopalisading necrosis. Immunohistochemistry indicates positivity for CD99 and NCAM, while p53 staining is negative. The Ki-67 labeling index, indicative of proliferative activity, is around 8%. Margins are not explicitly mentioned, suggesting the primary focus is on in situ tumor evaluation. The MGMT promoter methylation assay returned negative, suggesting no benefit from alkylating agent therapy. Lymph node involvement is not applicable in this primary brain tumor scenario.

The pathological examination of the right parietal occipital lesion reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma, classified as WHO Grade IV, indicating a high-grade, malignant primary brain tumor. The resected specimens, received in both fresh and formalin-fixed formats, comprise multiple fragments of cream to tan colored tissue. The intraoperative frozen section analysis and touch preparation also confirm the diagnosis of glioblastoma. Microscopic inspection demonstrates the characteristic features of glioblastoma, including marked cellularity, pleomorphism, areas of necrosis, and microvascular proliferation. Margins of the resected tissue are not explicitly stated, and an evaluation of lymph node involvement is not applicable due to the central nervous system (CNS) location of the tumor. The clinical background includes a malignant neoplasm in the occipital lobe, for which a right craniotomy was performed to achieve maximal tumor resection.

The pathological evaluation of the brain biopsy reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma multiforme. The tumor is characterized by significant hypercellularity, marked cellular pleomorphism, frequent mitotic figures, pronounced vasculo-endothelial hyperplasia, and extensive areas of necrosis. These features are indicative of a high-grade malignancy. Immunohistochemical staining indicates a proliferative index of approximately 40% as measured by MIB-1, suggesting an aggressive growth pattern. Additionally, some regions exhibit histological patterns consistent with oligodendroglioma, suggesting that the glioblastoma may have evolved from a pre-existing oligodendroglioma. Margin status and lymph node involvement were not described in the specimen, which is typical for primary brain tumors. The pathological findings support a diagnosis of advanced glioblastoma multiforme with evidence of histological progression from a lower-grade glioma.

The pathology report elucidates a diagnosis of invasive ductal carcinoma (IDC) of the left breast. The tumor is of histologic grade III/III and nuclear grade II/III, with an associated component of ductal carcinoma in situ (DCIS) exhibiting solid type features, intermediate nuclear grade, and moderate necrosis. DCIS constitutes up to 25% of the total tumor mass. Notably, the invasive carcinoma is located in the upper outer quadrant and central area of the breast. Margin analysis revealed no involvement of the nipple, surgical margins, or skin by the carcinoma. However, there is evidence of vascular invasion. Immunohistochemically, the invasive carcinoma demonstrates strong estrogen receptor positivity (95% nuclear staining) and moderate progesterone receptor positivity (60% nuclear staining), with a negative HER2 status. The lymphatic spread is evidenced by metastatic carcinoma involvement in sentinel lymph nodes #1 and #3, with no extranodal extension observed. Sentinel lymph node #2 and nine additional axillary lymph nodes are benign, indicating no further metastatic involvement at these sites. The non-neoplastic breast tissue shows biopsy site changes, stromal fibrosis, adenosis, and fibrocystic changes. This comprehensive pathological assessment highlights the aggressive nature of the primary tumor while providing a clear status of lymphatic spread and receptor profile, guiding further clinical management.

The pathological diagnosis reveals a glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV) in the right temporal lobe of the brain, characterized by gemistocytic astrocytic features, vascular proliferation, and pseudopalisading necrosis. Immunohistochemistry indicates positivity for CD99 and NCAM, while p53 staining is negative. The Ki-67 labeling index, indicative of proliferative activity, is around 8%. Margins are not explicitly mentioned, suggesting the primary focus is on in situ tumor evaluation. The MGMT promoter methylation assay returned negative, suggesting no benefit from alkylating agent therapy. Lymph node involvement is not applicable in this primary brain tumor scenario.

The pathological analysis reveals a diagnosis of glioblastoma in the right frontal brain region following a craniotomy and tumor resection. The tumor is characterized by extensive infiltration and effacement of cerebral tissue, notable for microvascular proliferation, extensive necrosis, and frequent mitotic figures, displaying features consistent with astrocytic neoplastic proliferation and pseudopalisading. Areas of hemorrhage with early organization are also observed. Immunohistochemical findings show a MIB1 proliferation index of 6%, focal glial fibrillogenesis demonstrated by GFAP, and overexpression of p53 in approximately 25% of the neoplastic nuclei, further corroborating the diagnosis of glioblastoma. The tissue sections examined include multiple irregular fragments of gray-tan to gray-white brain tissue. Special stains were applied to better characterize the tumor, with further detailed immunohistologic interpretations provided as an addendum. Margins, as well as additional lymph node involvement, are not explicitly detailed in the current findings.
